summary: I was born in East Cleveland, Cuyahoga County, Ohio, on October 28, 1920. Later, my father moved our family out to East Cleveland after he married my mother in 1919, and he stopped taking night calls as a doctor. East Cleveland was really the place where I grew up and experienced much of my early family life.

Ohio has been central to my family's story for generations. My father practiced medicine in Cleveland, Ohio, and his grandfather originally settled in Ohio in 1833 after emigrating from England. The Western Reserve and Sharon Township in Ohio were early places where my ancestors established themselves.

My middle name, Washington, comes from George Washington. My father was born on George Washington's birthday, and I was named after him, carrying on that connection. It's a small but meaningful family story tied to that great historical figure.
cues: father born on George Washington's birthday

summary: John Daykin was my paternal grandfather. I remember his name clearly, though I didn't have much personal interaction with him. He was part of the family lineage that came from England and settled in Ohio.

Ann Daykin was my paternal grandmother. Like my grandfather John Daykin, she was part of the family that emigrated from England and settled in Ohio. I know her name but have no detailed memories of her.

Thomas Kelly was my maternal grandfather. I remember his name, but I didn't know my maternal grandmother well as she passed away when I was very young. My mother's side of the family was rooted in Cleveland, Ohio.

England is where my great-grandfather and his family originally lived before emigrating to America. In 1833, they sold everything they had in England and made the journey to what was then the Western Reserve in Ohio. This move laid the foundation for our family's American roots.

The Western Reserve was the area in Ohio where my ancestors settled after leaving England in 1833. Specifically, they bought land in Sharon Township, which was part of the Western Reserve of Connecticut. This was the beginning of our family's life in America.

Sharon Township, Ohio, was where my great-grandfather and his family bought land after emigrating from England in 1833. They cleared the land for small farms, starting our family's agricultural roots in the area. It was part of the Western Reserve and an important place in our family history.

The Cleveland Clinic was a place that recognized my father's medical expertise. Although he was a general practitioner in Cleveland, the Clinic would refer their inoperable cases to him because of his skill in internal medicine. They would even call him in to consult on difficult cases, which shows the respect he earned in the medical community.

My father was a friend of Senator Mark Hanna and was involved politically during the 1900 presidential campaign. He helped carry Theodore Roosevelt's coat in northeastern Ohio, meaning he supported Roosevelt by providing insider information during the campaign. Although my father didn't pursue politics himself, he was connected to influential figures like Mark Hanna.

The 1900 presidential election was a significant political event my father was involved in. He supported William McKinley for president and Theodore Roosevelt for vice president, helping Roosevelt during the campaign in northeastern Ohio. This was a time when my father was politically active, though he never sought office himself.

William McKinley was the presidential candidate my father supported in the 1900 election. My father was active in the campaign, especially in northeastern Ohio, where he helped promote McKinley and his running mate, Theodore Roosevelt. McKinley's presidency was an important backdrop to my father's political involvement.

Theodore Roosevelt was the vice-presidential candidate in 1900, and my father played a role in his campaign in northeastern Ohio. He 'carried Roosevelt's coat,' meaning he helped Roosevelt by providing information about important people coming to events. Roosevelt's campaign was a memorable part of my father's political life.

Cleveland was the city where my father practiced medicine as a general practitioner, focusing on internal medicine. My mother was also born in Cleveland in 1879 and later taught at East High School there. Cleveland was central to much of my family's life and history.

East High School in Cleveland was where my mother worked as a schoolteacher. She taught Latin and algebra there, which was quite notable for a woman in that era. Her role as a teacher at East High School was an important part of her life before and after marriage.
